trajectory_summary: The call opens as a warm service check-in and converts into a tentative battery upsell callback — a genuine outcome the rep created by recognizing an opening in Dave's production confusion. However, the path was inefficient (four passes on an unanswerable technical question before the pivot), the cost objection was handled with explanation rather than inversion, and the button-up was incomplete: no verbal handshake, no text follow-up promise, and the closer's name is ambiguous in the transcript. The appointment is real but fragile — Dave said 'just to see,' not 'I'm interested.'
outcome_dispute: The v1 tag is 'unknown.' The call ends with a tentative callback booked for Tuesday at 11AM with a closer ([Tate / Kate — name unclear from transcript]). This should be tagged 'tentative_appointment' — not a full setter-style booked sit, but a structured callback with a named consultant, a specific time, and customer verbal agreement. The fragility of the booking (Dave's 'just to see' framing, incomplete button-up) means it should be flagged as conditional rather than confirmed.

primary_coaching_focus.quote: K. Sounds good, mister Bennett. Appreciate your time. And, yeah, any other questions about the percentage is too, feel free to ask Tate about that.
primary_coaching_focus.why: The rep created a real appointment from a service call — that's a genuine win. But the booking is fragile: Dave said 'just to see,' the verbal handshake is missing, and there is no text follow-up promise. For a callback booked over a weekend, the text confirmation is the primary artifact keeping the appointment alive. The rep did the hard work of creating the opportunity and then left the two most retention-critical elements on the table. This is the highest-leverage coaching gap on this call because it directly affects whether the rep gets paid for the sit.

key_moments.quote: So like I said, a lot of it is because, you know, on those days when it is producing, a lot of that gets used up in real time. So the, as soon as it's producing, the house is just using it right away. So it can't get down to those levels if it's just being used up. Does that make sense?
key_moments.stewart_read: The rep's explanation is plausible but may be incomplete. Dave is reading a percentage from his monitoring app — it's unclear whether that percentage represents grid dependency, battery state of charge, or something else entirely. The rep never asked what app or display Dave is reading, which means the explanation may be answering the wrong question. This is a knowledge gap that compounds across four re-asks: the rep is confidently explaining a mechanism that may not match what Dave is actually observing.

key_moments.quote: am I banking? What is going to the electric company?
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the earliest and clearest setup for the ComEd buyback frame. Dave is explicitly asking about net metering — whether his excess production is being credited back. This was the moment to introduce the 70% buyback reality and pivot to battery storage. Instead, the rep answered the mechanics question ('excess gets sent back to the grid') without surfacing the buyback rate gap. The battery upsell didn't arrive until 06:01 — nearly two minutes later — after Dave had already moved on to a new sub-question.

key_moments.quote: No. I am never really looked into that. Okay. Would it be worth I I mean, it's very expensive. I know the batteries ain't cheap. So would that be worth it?
key_moments.stewart_read: Dave raises a cost objection on batteries immediately after the rep introduces the product. The rep's response at 06:42 is an explanation of battery benefits — what they store, when they help, how they work with ComEd's buyback rate. This is a features-and-benefits answer to a cost objection. The inversion available here: Dave is already paying the cost of NOT having a battery every month through the ComEd buyback gap the rep just described. The rep had the frame (70% buyback) but used it as a feature explanation rather than a cost flip. Per coaching_philosophy.objection_inversion: 'What's expensive is NOT switching.' The rep's answer was not wrong — it was just the safe move when the high-leverage move was available.

key_moments.quote: I know I got a tree in front, but since then, I have trimmed the tree, so it'll get more sunlight.
key_moments.stewart_read: Dave has proactively removed a shading obstacle to improve his system's production. This is a strong engagement signal — he's invested enough in the system to take physical action to optimize it. The rep walked past this entirely, continuing the production-explanation loop. This signal also has cross-sell relevance: a customer who trimmed a tree to improve solar production is a customer who cares about maximizing output — exactly the profile for a battery storage conversation. The rep eventually got to the battery pitch, but without anchoring it to Dave's demonstrated investment in the system.
